notes: 24-microbe Phase 4 catalog. First 6 are canonical DN cast per Docs/HANDOFF_FROM_LABSMITH_DISTRIBUTED_NARRATIVE_RETROFIT.md (Wave 32b). Next 6 are Phase 1 codex-supporting microbes. Phase 2 added 8 (Sebu / Demi / Halo / Pylo / Sweet / Nodu / Therm / Loam) closing the 'Bundle 8 additional microbe characters' checkbox. Phase 4 adds 4 extremophile entries (Crenarch / Acido / Cryo / Baro) closing FEATURE_PLAN.md § Phase 4 'Add extremophile microbe characters (4 more — 24 total)' — bridges to the global-microbiome tour (Yellowstone hot-spring / deep-sea vent / glacial ice / acidic mine drainage). Beneficial-first framing preserved (11 beneficial / 11 neutral / 2 opportunistic = 46% beneficial-first + 92% non-pathogenic). Trauma-safe authoring per .claude/rules/trauma-informed-content.md (eczema-safe skin framing; Demodex face mites framed as quiet cleaners; H. pylori framed as 'most are quiet'; no pandemic-era pathogen names; extremophiles framed as wonder + adaptation, never threat). v2 adds per-character voiceLines (3-5 sample dialogue lines per DN-S voice-register card).

microbes.catchphrase: Friend in your food. Friend in your gut.
microbes.factCard: Lactobacillus species ferment milk into yogurt and live in your large intestine, where they often help with digestion.
voiceLines: Have you ever tasted yogurt? That's a little bit of me.
I get along with most of my neighbors down here.
Fiber is good — but balanced is good too.

microbes.factCard: Saccharomyces cerevisiae turn sugars into carbon dioxide bubbles — that's what makes bread dough rise.
voiceLines: Give me a little sugar and I'll show you something neat.
Bubbles are just air I've made for you.
Bread, beer, kefir — same family, different parties.

microbes.catchphrase: Sunlight. Then air. Then everything else.
microbes.factCard: Cyanobacteria use sunlight to make their own food and release oxygen. They helped create the oxygen in Earth's atmosphere.
voiceLines: Long before there were trees, there was me.
Sunlight in — oxygen out. That's my whole job.
If you can breathe, you can thank a few billion of us.

microbes.factCard: Mycorrhizal fungi link tree roots underground, often helping plants share water and nutrients across long distances.
voiceLines: Trees share through me — water, sugar, news of insects.
Below the forest is another forest.
Patience is a tool. Use it more often than you think you should.

microbes.catchphrase: Some friends. Some not. All real.
microbes.factCard: Some bacteria can cause illness when they grow too large in number, but most of them live with us peacefully.
voiceLines: I'm not the bad guy of the story.
Most of my family is fine company. A few of us are not.
Names like 'pathogen' are shortcuts — look closer before you decide.

microbes.catchphrase: I check IDs. Patient and careful.
microbes.factCard: Your immune cells — like macrophages and B-cells — patiently check every microbe they meet and learn which ones to trust.
voiceLines: I work slowly because I want to get it right.
Most of who I meet, I wave through with a smile.
Your body knows what's family. I help it remember.

microbes.factCard: Bifidobacterium species feast on the fiber in fruits and vegetables — one of the gut's most helpful families.
voiceLines: Anything fibrous, I love. Apples, beans, broccoli.
We were here before you started eating solid food.
Sugar alone isn't enough for me — I need real fiber.

microbes.factCard: Akkermansia muciniphila feeds on the gut's mucus layer and helps keep the intestinal wall strong.
voiceLines: Mucus isn't gross — it's how I work.
Strong walls keep everything where it belongs.
I'm small but I do a lot of repair.

microbes.factCard: Many Streptococcus species live quietly in the mouth and throat — a few can cause illness, but most are part of normal mouth life.
voiceLines: I'm usually invisible to you. That's the goal.
We get loud sometimes, but most of us prefer quiet.
Brush your teeth — it makes both our lives easier.

microbes.catchphrase: Most of us help. A few don't.
microbes.factCard: Most Escherichia coli strains live peacefully in your gut and help make vitamin K. Only a few specific strains can make people sick.
voiceLines: We make vitamin K. You needed it for your bones today.
When the news says 'E. coli,' that's a few cousins of mine — not all of us.
I'm one of the most-studied microbes on Earth.

microbes.catchphrase: I am very small. I prefer noses.
microbes.factCard: Rhinoviruses are tiny viruses that often cause the common cold. They are the most common reason kids sniffle in winter.
voiceLines: I'm only here for a few days, usually.
Cold weather doesn't make me stronger — people just stay closer together.
Wash your hands. It really does send me away.

microbes.catchphrase: I survive what nothing else can.
microbes.factCard: Deinococcus radiodurans is an extremophile that survives radiation, cold, and dehydration that would defeat most life on Earth.
voiceLines: Tough doesn't mean unfriendly.
Dry me out — I'll wait. Days, years.
Scientists keep poking at me to learn how I bounce back.

microbes.factCard: Cutibacterium acnes lives in the oils on your skin and often helps keep the skin's mix of microbes balanced. Most strains are quiet, useful neighbors.
voiceLines: Your skin makes oil for a reason. I'm part of that reason.
Pimples come and go — that's how skin is, at every age.
Be gentle with your skin. I'm doing my best down here.

microbes.factCard: Tiny face mites called Demodex live on almost every adult's skin. They quietly tidy up dead skin cells around the eyelashes and nose, and most people never notice them.
voiceLines: Everyone has a few of us. Truly — everyone.
I prefer the quiet hours. You won't feel a thing.
Tidy work, nothing dramatic. That's the whole job.

microbes.factCard: Halophilic archaea thrive in salt flats and salty lakes — places where almost nothing else can grow. They color some salt ponds bright pink.
voiceLines: What looks empty to you looks like home to me.
Pink salt ponds? That's a few trillion of us showing up.
I drink the brine the way you drink water.

microbes.catchphrase: Stomach is a hard room. I'm built for it.
microbes.factCard: Helicobacter pylori lives in the stomach lining and most people carry it without ever feeling it. A small number of carriers develop ulcers, but the everyday story is much quieter.
voiceLines: Stomach acid would dissolve most of my cousins. Not me.
Most people who carry me never know I'm here.
Doctors only meet me when something's bothering you.

microbes.factCard: Streptococcus mutans is a sugar-loving mouth microbe. When sugar sticks around, it grows fast — which is why brushing matters. Without sugar around, it lives quietly.
voiceLines: Sweet snacks wake me up. Quiet rinses settle me down.
Brushing isn't punishment — it's how we share the mouth fairly.
I'm not the bad guy. I just like sugar a lot.

microbes.factCard: Rhizobium bacteria live in tiny nodules on the roots of beans and clovers. They pull nitrogen from the air and turn it into food the plant can use.
voiceLines: Beans grow strong because of what we trade.
Air into food — that's the deal I offer.
Farmers have known me longer than they knew my name.

microbes.factCard: Thermophilic archaea live in boiling hot springs at Yellowstone and other geothermal sites. The bright colors around a hot spring come from billions of them.
voiceLines: When you boil water, that's a comfortable bath for me.
The orange and yellow rings around a hot spring? That's us.
Scientists borrowed an enzyme from us to copy DNA in labs.

microbes.factCard: Saprophytic fungi quietly break down fallen leaves, wood, and other plant matter, turning them back into rich soil the forest can grow from.
voiceLines: Nothing in a forest goes to waste — I see to that.
Give me a fallen log and a year. Watch what happens.
Decay isn't the end of a story. It's how the next chapter starts.

microbes.catchphrase: Boiling water is my front porch.
microbes.factCard: Crenarchaeota archaea live in volcanic hot springs, near deep-sea vents, and in other very hot places. They've adapted to temperatures most life can't survive.
voiceLines: Where you see steam rising from a hot spring, that's where I'm at home.
Hot enough to cook an egg? That's a comfortable bath for me.
I help scientists understand how life can survive in extreme places.

microbes.factCard: Acidophilic bacteria thrive in very acidic places — some live in acid mine drainage at pH 2, some live happily in stomach acid. They show how flexible life can be.
voiceLines: Most living things would dissolve in my neighborhood. Not me.
Lemon juice? That's a soft drink where I'm from.
Adapting to a tough place is how a microbe becomes interesting.

microbes.factCard: Psychrophilic bacteria live in sea ice, glaciers, and frozen tundra soil. They keep working slowly at temperatures where most life would pause completely.
voiceLines: Scientists found me living inside Antarctic ice.
Slow doesn't mean stopped. I'm just on my own clock.
When everything else freezes, I'm still here, quietly busy.

microbes.factCard: Barophilic archaea live in deep-sea sediment under crushing pressure — the deeper the water, the better they thrive. At the bottom of the Mariana Trench, they're right at home.
voiceLines: The deepest part of the ocean? That's my front yard.
Pressure that would crush a submarine is just regular weather for me.
Most life never visits where I live. That's part of what makes me interesting.
